title: Contract Security Vulnerabilities
description: Security vulnerabilities in smart contracts can lead to fund loss or functional anomalies, such as reentrancy attacks, overflow vulnerabilities, etc.

title: Honeypot Contract Detection
description: Difficulty in identifying contracts with honeypot logic, which may be designed to attract investors but restrict withdrawals.

title: Mint Permission Control
description: Improperly set Mint permissions in contracts may lead to unlimited token minting, diluting existing holders' equity.

title: Owner Blacklisting Permission
description: Contract owners may have the permission to blacklist users at any time, restricting users' access to their assets.

title: Low Audit Efficiency
description: Traditional audit methods are time-consuming and labor-intensive, unable to quickly respond to market changes and emerging security threats.

title: Lack of Real-time Monitoring
description: Inability to real-time monitor contract status and potential security risks, making it difficult to timely detect and respond to issues.

title: Insufficient Gas Optimization
description: Contract code has not undergone sufficient Gas optimization, leading to high user transaction costs.

title: Yield Farming Risks
description: DeFi yield farms have smart contract risks that may lead to principal loss.

title: Token Value Dilution
description: Improper token economic models may lead to token value dilution, affecting investor returns.

title: Market Volatility Impact
description: Smart contracts may exhibit abnormal behavior under extreme market volatility, affecting user asset security.

title: Reentrancy Attack Risks
description: Smart contracts may suffer reentrancy attacks when handling external calls, leading to funds being maliciously withdrawn.

title: Integer Overflow Vulnerabilities
description: Integer operations in smart contracts may lead to overflow or underflow, thereby causing security issues.

title: Access Control Issues
description: Lack of proper access control mechanisms in contracts may allow unauthorized users to perform sensitive operations.

title: Front-running Attacks
description: Attackers exploit the predictability of transaction order to execute their transactions before users' transactions.

title: Flash Loan Attacks
description: Attackers use the instantaneous capital advantage of flash loans to manipulate prices or execute complex attack strategies.

title: Oracle Manipulation
description: Attackers manipulate price data provided by oracles, thereby affecting smart contracts that rely on this data.

title: Rug Pull Schemes
description: Project teams suddenly withdraw funds after attracting sufficient capital, causing investor losses.

title: Malicious Code Injection
description: Contracts contain hidden malicious code that executes harmful operations under specific conditions.

title: Insufficient Testing
description: Smart contracts are not fully tested before deployment, leading to potential vulnerabilities not being discovered.

title: Dependency Vulnerabilities
description: External libraries or dependencies used by smart contracts have security vulnerabilities that may be exploited by attackers.

title: Slow Audit Process
description: Traditional security audit processes take a long time, which may delay project launch time.

title: High Audit Costs
description: Professional security audit services are expensive, posing a financial burden on small projects.

title: Manual Code Review
description: Relying on manual code review is inefficient and prone to missing potential issues.

title: Lack of Automation Tools
description: Lack of automated security scanning tools leads to inefficient audit processes.

title: Complex Contract Logic
description: Smart contract logic is too complex, increasing audit difficulty and time costs.

title: Cross-chain Compatibility
description: Differences between different blockchain platforms require contracts to be adapted multiple times, increasing development and audit costs.

title: Gas Price Volatility
description: Gas prices fluctuate significantly during network congestion, affecting contract execution costs and user experience.

title: High Transaction Failure Rate
description: Smart contract transactions often fail due to insufficient Gas or other reasons, affecting user experience.

title: Scalability Issues
description: Smart contracts may face scalability challenges under high load, leading to transaction delays.

title: Debugging Difficulties
description: Smart contracts are difficult to debug and modify after deployment, increasing the difficulty of problem resolution.

title: Low Yield Opportunities

title: Impermanent Loss

title: Protocol Risk

title: Liquidity Risks
